carousels.caption:
De Beers just dug 88% more diamonds — and earned 44% less for them.

The Q2 production report: 7.8 million carats out of the ground, up 88% on a year ago when the miner was throttling supply to defend prices. First-half output hit 14.9M carats, up 46%. Botswana did the heavy lifting, raising output 107%.

The catch is the price line. The average rough price fell 32% to $105 a carat, and revenue dropped 44% to $665 million. This is a business running back toward capacity — straight into the softest diamond market in a generation. The price-defense era is over; the volume era is back, and it's meeting weak demand.

Full breakdown on the trade paper — link in bio.

Gold spent Friday doing what it's done all July: defending a line.

Spot bullion held near $4,028 an ounce — a slim margin above the $4,000 level that's become the market's psychological floor — after sliding from a two-week peak above $4,160.

It's caught between two forces pulling hard in opposite directions. The haven bid: Brent crude pushed past $100 a barrel on the widening Iran conflict and strikes on Gulf shipping. The drag: the ten-year yield reached 4.70%, and traders now price an 80% chance of a September rate rise, up from 68%. One says buy gold; the other says don't. For now, $4,000 holds.

The gap between a natural diamond and its lab-grown twin has become a canyon — and it changed who wins the engagement ring.

Analysing appraisal and insurance records from 2019 to 2025, BriteCo found lab-grown now priced ~73% below comparable naturals. In 2019 that discount was just 27%. A one-carat natural averages ~$4,200 in the data; its grown equivalent, about $1,000 or less.

Here's the twist: as grown got cheaper, natural stones reclaimed 57% of engagement rings — the exact reversal lab-grown was supposed to prevent. Relentless Chinese and Indian output keeps pushing grown prices down, and a good made in ever-greater volume struggles to hold luxury meaning. Cheap won the value buyer; rarity won the ring.
